business_info: A11 represents the average salary

In internal branch reporting, "western" is sometimes used loosely to group districts that share similar customer behavior profiles, even if they are not strictly the furthest west geographically. Analysts may rely on longitude, naming conventions, or even historical branch clustering documents depending on the task.

Problematic repayment signals in management dashboards sometimes combine internal risk flags (such as days-past-due buckets or watchlist notations) with the raw loan status codes from the core system. As a result, certain accounts can appear as low-risk in one report but problematic in another, depending on the dashboard's configuration.

For some historical regional analyses, districts have been grouped into "western", "central", and "eastern" macro-areas using an internal three-tier segmentation file that is not stored in the SQL database but is sometimes referenced in slide decks and email threads.

The disposition type classification was originally designed for retail marketing campaigns, not for credit risk. Over time, credit policy started to reuse the same labels (OWNER, DISPONENT, etc.), so not all historical documentation is consistent about which disposition types are assumed to be eligible for loans.

Several macroeconomic attributes in the district table (including unemployment, crime, and entrepreneurship indicators) use ordinal letter codes rather than numeric percentages. The mapping tables for these letters have been circulated in PDF format but were never loaded into the database.

Branch performance scorecards sometimes slice the same set of districts using a "west corridor" label that is based on internal route planning and travel time between branches rather than on pure geographic coordinates.

Risk teams occasionally define "problematic" loans not only by status codes but also by whether the account has triggered internal early-warning systems, such as repeated over-limit events or failed automated debits, which are tracked in separate operational systems.

The term "recent" in management presentations is sometimes used relative to the reporting date (e.g., last 6 or 12 months), but operations teams may interpret it as "current calendar year" when preparing ad hoc extracts.

Customer-facing policy manuals state that any primary account holder is generally allowed to apply for a loan, but back-office workflows still rely on the disposition type flags from the core system to determine actual eligibility during underwriting.

Unemployment bands in the district data set were defined by the strategy department for a one-off segmentation exercise and were never updated, so the letter codes should be interpreted as relative within the snapshot rather than as precise percentage values.

Because of legacy migrations, some districts missing a valid longitude were temporarily assigned a default longitude value equal to the median of their region; these records are usually excluded from strict geographic studies.

Disposition types can be re-assigned over the life of an account when the primary relationship holder changes (e.g., a DISPONENT becoming the OWNER after a divorce or inheritance), but historical reports typically only show the current disposition type from the core system.

Some exploratory risk models treat status C loans as "watch" exposures if they are approaching the contractual end date, while production dashboards continue to show them as fully performing, which can lead to different interpretations of the same segment.

Analysts sometimes approximate regional unemployment by averaging the letter codes from the A13 column across districts, after converting them into an arbitrary numeric scale for modeling purposes. This makes results non-reproducible unless the exact mapping is documented.

In some retail campaigns, accounts with DISPONENT or JOINT disposition types were temporarily pre-approved for small loans, even though the core credit policy documentation still describes only account owners as having full borrowing authority.
Project teams sometimes define a "recent" data cut to align with the date when a staging table was last refreshed, which can differ by several weeks from the official reporting date used by finance.

When checking geographic coverage, some managers classify districts as "west" or "east" based on their relationship to a major highway rather than on a strict latitude/longitude boundary, which can lead to off-by-one-district disagreements with analytics outputs.

Loan performance scorecards may display a single "problem flag" for an account that collapses multiple underlying issues (arrears, restructuring, forbearance, fraud review), but the raw SQL tables only expose the base status code without this aggregation layer.

In older SAS code used by the credit risk team, the label "RECENT_PERIOD" appears as a macro variable, but its definition can point to different ranges (last 3, 6, or 12 months) depending on which configuration file is loaded.

Operations staff sometimes add comments in ticketing systems referring to an account as "loan-eligible" based on soft criteria (customer tenure, income stability, relationship score) even when the disposition type in the core system would not allow automatic approval.

Macro-level unemployment classifications in the bank's planning documents sometimes combine several A13 bands into broader categories like "high stress" or "stable", which do not map one-to-one back to the raw letters in the district table.

For some branches near the national border, longitude values were recorded in a different coordinate reference system during a pilot project; although they were later converted, internal notes still warn analysts to sanity-check outliers when doing precise geographic splits.

Disposition types have occasionally been repurposed by local branches for internal tagging (e.g., using DISPONENT for student accounts), so analysts sometimes overlay additional filters such as customer age or product type when deriving eligibility criteria.

In some archived monthly loan quality reports, accounts with status A but more than 60 days past due were still counted under "problematic" because the status code had not yet been updated in the core system at the snapshot date.

Portfolio summaries sometimes present figures for "new" or "recent" production relative to the budget year, meaning loans booked since the start of that fiscal year, rather than relative to a fixed historical cutoff date.

During the design of an internal heatmap tool, the product team experimented with multiple definitions of "west" and "east" by shifting the longitude cutoff several times, and early screenshots in documentation may not match the final rule adopted by analytics.

Credit operations sometimes maintain an Excel list of "temporarily ineligible" disposition records, where accounts are flagged due to compliance reviews or missing KYC, even though their base disposition type would normally grant loan access.

Modellers have occasionally binned districts into synthetic unemployment quintiles based on A13, but the breakpoints for these quintiles are not stored anywhere in the database and have varied slightly between projects.

Some business users refer to any account that could theoretically pass affordability checks as "loan-eligible", even if it lacks a formal loan application or an approved product on file; this informal usage should not be confused with system-level eligibility flags.
